How to Create
Irresistible Sales
Messages for ANY
Business
Module 3: The Psychology of
Customers, Copywriting and Selling
With Dan Kennedy and John Carlton
www.gkic.com
www.getwsodownload.com

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 4
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
The Psychology of Customers, Copywriting and Selling
Dan Kennedy: Let's talk around copywriting. The first thing you and I agreed that
we would talk about is some underlying fundamental insight into the psychology
of the buyer, the person we are actually trying to get to read our copy, respond to
our copy, buy things from us, etc.
What's your overarching position on who this person is and where they start?
John Carlton: I accidentally got a degree in psychology, a four year degree from
the University of California and I graduated into the teeth of the '74 recession
and absolutely nothing I learned there was of any value.
There were 20,000 BAs in psychology given out in the United States that year
for one job that was available. I wound up as a childcare worker in Sacramento
for a couple of years.
So I took an interest in psychology into my life as
a freelance copywriter and hung out with the
streetwise salesmen and really paid attention to
old school psychology.
When I started writing direct response
advertising, it was a very short move for me to
go from looking at the audiences out there in a
target market and then looking beneath the
surface and looking at what I believed they needed to have happen to be able to
move them off of their inaction.
Your Job as a Writer
When I described it later, when I started writing about this, I described people
in the average market as a “somnambulistic blob welded to the couch so firmly
it wouldn't leave to save its ass if the house was burning down.”
Your job as an ad
writer is to get them
to take out their
wallet and actually
take some action.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 5
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
Your job as an ad writer is to get them to take out their wallet and actually take
some action.
This is what I was talking about in the previous session about how easy it can be
to get somebody up on the fence, to get somebody to agree that, “Yeah you’ve
got a good product and maybe someday down the road I'll buy it.”
But you've got to get them to act and that's where
the pro is separated from the rookie.
Dan: That's why, and I'm reasonably certain you agree, that's why I push people to
study the old direct response so much.
I say “there's gold in the old” because they had a much tougher job at what he just
described really then we do now.
Because they actually did have to get them up off the couch.
Somebody had to go get a checkbook out of the kitchen drawer, write out a check,
fill out a coupon, put it in an envelope, not talk themselves out of it, get a postage
stamp and drop it in a mail box in order to respond.
Then, finally, they were able to respond by phone with 800 numbers and the ability
to take a credit card over the phone. But that's more recent than it is ancient.
Joe Sugarman was the first guy to put an 800 number in a direct response ad and
be able to take orders by credit card over the phone.
That was early ‘70s. I know for some of you that's ancient history but not for John
and I and not for many. It's not like it was at the turn of the century for God’s
sakes.
So those people doing direct response then had a much higher hurdle to move this
person John just described, and I generally share the same disdainful idea of where
they are.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 6
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
John: Well there's a subtle balance there. Don't get the idea that I don't like my
customer. I like him a lot.
Salesmen Lead Better Lives
In fact one of the things that I try to teach people who come to me for advice just
on life and stuff is that salesmen lead better lives.
The people who know how to sell and understand what Dan and I are talking
about, the ability to get somebody to move and actually buy something, you lead
a better life because you can't do that if you don't live firmly in reality.
The vast majority of people live on the surface of life, bobbing around like
flotsam and jetsam on the surface of the ocean, taken hither and to by the waves.
Entrepreneurs who are good salesmen actually
start to control your life:
 You have goals.
 You go forward.
 You are the engine of your success.
 You move yourself forward.
That is not shared by the vast majority of people
out there. They are living in very shallow lives.
Salesmen lead better lives all the way around.
You don't look at the world the way you wish it was and the way you think it
ought to be. You look at the world the way it is.
People get shocked when they first start thinking about this because they think
“Well, does that mean I have to despise my customer base?”
No, you actually start to love people more when all of the nonsense falls away,
all the B.S. is taken away and you're actually dealing with people for who they
People who know
how to sell lead a
better life because
you can't do that if
you don't live firmly
in reality.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 7
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
are and how they will actually act as opposed to the way you think they should
or ought to act.
It changes the whole dynamic.
Customer Feedback
Dan: And where they start. Sales people learn not to start at a different place than
the prospect is because you get feedback. You sit across the kitchen table, you sit
across the desk in the office and, if they're over there and you start here, you pretty
quickly get a feel of that and you back up and
retrench.
We don't get that feedback when we sell through
media. A very common mistake is making
assumptions about where they are and starting
further along than they are in the process towards
making a decision about whatever it is that you
are putting in front of them.
Like what you just said, that we have our own engines and our engines start pretty
quick; they either don't have their own engine or it's still got a hand crank. We’ve
got to start way back with them there, not like ready to hear the good news about
the thing we have to offer them.
John: Dan, do you remember the first time you met somebody who was a
customer who you'd not met before and they were a little astonished that you
didn't know them because they felt they knew you so well?
Dan: Yes. Which is what's supposed to happen, right?
John: The moment it happens, you realize now you’ve arrived as a marketer
because the communication that you just effected with them might’ve been a
year ago and they bought and then you’ve got more things going back and forth.
Sales people learn
not to start at a
different place than
the prospect is
because you get
feedback.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 8
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
They’re reading your newsletters, they’re reading your stuff. They feel that
connection on a visceral level. Many people feel they know Dan after reading
him, before they’ve met him.
That’s a major step in moving from being a social entrepreneur to having the
unlimited potential.
Gun to the Head
I’d like to add one other side to that psychology
thing that I had. For myself, I operated from a
“gun to the head” position and I found out later
that Halbert did the same thing and Jay Abraham
did the same thing. I don’t know if you did.
But I had no background. I’d never met a freelancer when I became one. I was
making up the gig as I was going along.
I was one of the few freelancers down in Los Angeles in the ‘80s because it just
wasn’t an occupation that was well thought of or even that a lot of people knew
about. So I had to make up things as I was going.
One of the things I did was I imagined a gun was to my head when I was writing
an ad that would go off if the ad didn’t work and how would I react to that?
 Would I choose that headline?
 Would I take that tack in my sales pitch?
 Would I tell a joke or would I be straightforward?
Over and over again that idea of “my life is on the line if this ad
doesn’t work,” kept me really focused on classic salesmanship.
So I didn’t experiment because, when it came down to it, writing for a client, I
already had the money or I was going to get paid whether the ad worked or not.
A lot of writers can get lazy.
I imagined a gun
was to my head
when I was writing.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 9
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
Rather than that, I kept thinking, “What’s the one thing I would do if my life
really, really depended on that?”
So the gun to the head philosophy of working erases a lot of the B.S. that can
leak into your writing when you think, “Why not try this?” Well, because it won’t
work.
Quantity of Output v Quality
Dan: One of the evils of the Internet is that it has caused marketers to
dramatically increase the quantity of their output to their customers and made
them therefore quick, sloppy and casual about a lot of it.
Really most people would be better off putting 75% less out
and really working on the 25% to get it
right.
John’s absolutely right and, of course, as a
freelancer, once you’re in the game for royalties,
not just fees, then, from a monetary standpoint,
you actually care whether or not this stuff works.
It’s not just from an ethical standpoint or a
professional reputation standpoint; I’m often more concerned that it works than
the client is even.
Yeah you work on it like this is life or death and this baby’s got to produce or else.
I think a lot of marketers do not do that. They are like, “Okay we’re going to get
two e-mails out a day this week and we’re going to do it every day for five days.
Let’s slap these babies together and let’s push the button.” I think it’s a mistake.
John: One of the things that I know you’ve come up against with clients, which I
do, is that you will write the ad that you believe needs to be run to do it and
everything that you know says this ad needs to be run.
You need to control
the situation and
you do that by
charging massive
amounts of money.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 10
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
What’s the first thing the client says? “My daughter the English Lit major read it
and thinks you ought to change the headline. Now use this word and do this.”
If you don’t have control as a freelancer…, one of the first things I teach
freelancers is to become the adult in the room. You control the situation.
You are not a vendor. You are in control.
You do that by charging massive amounts of
money.
Dan: And firing people.
John: One of the things I used to do in the
agencies is I’d walk in and say, “I won’t work
with you guys until you fire the designer.”
Because the designer had way too much say in what the ad looked like. He
wanted gray floating blocks of copy under his designs. But that was back in the
‘80s before direct response had re-established itself as a working medium.
Following the Formula
Dan: The chief point here is that there are reliable things to do and they are based
on behavioral psychology.
I say direct response is roughly half behavioral psych and half math. That’s really
what it is. There’s reliable psychological principles; there’s reliable sales principles.
Yes, somebody periodically innovates but it’s really not necessary to be the
innovator in order to make a lot of money in this field.
There’s a fairly predictable, reliable path of elements that
make things work and they really haven’t changed.
The media’s changed, technology’s changed but a presentation that works is a
presentation that works is a presentation that works.
Direct response is
roughly half
behavioral
psychology and
half math.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 11
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
John: And you and I, as writers, what were we just talking about in the hallway
before we came in? If we want to be creative we’ll go write a freaking novel and
be creative there.
Dan: Although it turns out, the truth be told, that’s not that creative of a process,
either. When I got in advertising I thought it was a creative exercise – and it is not
that at all. It is a methodical exercise. It turns out fiction is also methodical. There
are formulas. There are architectures. There are reliable things.
Circling the Desk
I’m going to shift gears and I’d like you to talk a little bit about the whole “circle
the desk” thing. What goes on really before we get a word on paper?
John: I did two things when I started writing for myself and really being a
freelancer is an entrepreneurial gig. It was my own business and I had to figure
out how to make sure I was doing it and doing it right.
I made a couple of decisions. One was business before pleasure which, for a
slacker, changed my life. I could still have a drink with the boys and party and do
whatever but I had to finish all the business first. So that was huge.
But then what I did is I started thinking, “Wait a
minute I need to really nail down this working
environment for me.” I needed every trick in the
book on my side.
So I actually wore writing clothes. How many
people have writing clothes or a hat or something that they do? You’ve heard
people talk about putting on your writer hat or putting on your salesman hat. I
literally did that and it helped.
Now one of the reasons it helped is because my writing clothes are dirty sweats
and an old t-shirt.
I’d put a different
hat on when I was
ready to sit down.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 12
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
So I actually couldn’t go to the door and answer it or walk outside. I was stuck in
there unless I was going to change clothes.
I actually had different hats so that I’d put a different hat on when I would call a
client; I’d put a different hat on when I was ready to sit down and actually face a
typewriter – that’s how old I freakin’ am – but, whether I was staring at a
monitor or blank sheet of paper.
What Dan was mentioning is there is this habit of getting into the mood to write.
I told you that writer’s block doesn’t exist in the
last session, but how many people have trouble
gearing up to sit down and actually write stuff
you need to write?
Dan: Yeah, it’s because your subconscious isn’t
ready.
John: And that’s why… what I started doing is, I like dogs... I noticed that dogs
circle things. They circle around before they lay down and I just started circling
the desk and I’d actually work myself into a froth.
I didn’t approach the desk until I was ready to write and that’s why I never
encountered writer’s block.
I had all my ducks in a row. I knew what I was going to do.
When I sat down I wasn’t thinking, “Well, where do I start?”
 I knew where to start.
 I knew who I was going after.
 I knew what the pain points were.
 I knew what the features/benefits were.
By the way, when I write, I usually write the bullets first and then move to a
headline and then start writing.
I didn’t approach the
desk until I was
ready to write.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 13
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
So I construct the ad kind of like building a house or remodeling a house.
But I would circle the desk and work myself into a froth and one of the things
that I thought when I was sitting down is I would think, “Goddammit, there are
people walking around out there today that have money in their wallet that
belongs to me. And the universe will not be right until I rectify that situation.”
Making Writing Easy
Dan: I hear from people all the time that writing is hard and, “How can I write a
newsletter once a month or a sales letter?”
If writing – and writing at a decent speed – is difficult for you,
it is because your preparation is poor.
It’s just that simple and one or both kinds of
preparation…
One is the pragmatic preparation of having a
profile of who you’re writing to; having some sort of template in place, having
some good swipe files until you get so old and accomplished at it like he and I are. I
imagine you, like me now, 90% of the time you’re working out of the swipe files in
your head, not the swipe files in the backroom – although they are still there.
So there’s that kind of preparation. Am I practically ready to write and then
there’s, “Is your subconscious in gear? Does it know that that’s what’s supposed to
be happening right now?”
Almost every copywriter has some sort of ritualistic behavior, not necessarily the
one he described, but some sort of ritualistic behavior. They have an environment.
Real copywriters aren’t writing at Starbucks. The world thinks that’s their office
somehow and Schultz has convinced everybody that that’s their office or Panera
Bread for God's sakes.
Real copywriters have an environment that they have organized ─ a place – and
not only is it because their stuff is there and they’re maybe protected from
Real copywriters
aren’t writing at
Starbucks.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 13 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 14
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
disruptions; it’s because their subconscious knows, when they enter that place,
“Okay I’ve got to flip open the drawers. I’ve got to turn on the switches. This is now
all about writing.”
So, when it’s difficult for you, it’s really a matter of preparation more than it is you
can’t write, writing is hard, you don’t have a good idea.
Developing Routines
John: Let me help some of you out who do have this trouble. Just go ahead and
develop your own routines. You can do whatever you want.
There are two kinds of writers – those who work in complete silence; I work in
complete silence – and those who play music in the background. Steven King
wrote with AC/DC blaring.
Dan: I have the TV on. I don’t see it.
John: Exactly. It’s like white noise. So don’t
worry about that. Choose either one or the other
but get your routine down so that you do that.
Take the phone off the hook and make a big deal
about taking the phone off the hook.
For your routine, do whatever you need to do but get the routine down. It can be
a two minute routine or a five minute routine or whatever you do but set it
down.
But make it so that nobody’s going to disturb you and then set a clock. This was
a big breakthrough for a lot of people.
Dan: This is big. This is very big.
John: Start with 50 minutes out of an hour. Work for two hours with 10 minute
breaks. So work for 50 minutes and then take a 10 minute break and literally
leave.
Do whatever you
need to do but get
the routine down.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 14 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 15
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
Get out of that and, when you come back, do your routine again to get back into
it.
Dan: We’re stylistically different on that. I take no breaks.
John: But find out.
Dan: Right. But I write to a deadline just like you do. If you don’t have a stop
point, if you don’t have a stop point that you’re working toward, your subconscious
just doesn’t know when it needs to be done.
John: It’s astonishing what an alarm clock… and I
would get an old school alarm clock or you can
use your iPhone – it goes off and it does that little
drum beat or whatever.
But find out, and it could be you’re 45 minutes
and 15 minutes, it could be you’re half an hour
and half an hour. Mine’s more like two hours and
then half an hour and then back for two hours and
then I’m done.
If you’re steady productive in four hours of writing, you’d get more done than
the average marketer gets done in a month.
Dan: But you want to dictate end times and I dictate the amount of work that’s
going to be done by that end time.
So, if it’s 16 pages and it’s been assigned 50 minutes, my entire system now knows
that it’s got 50 minutes to do 16 pages.
John: One of the big things I remember, that you cemented down, I was kind of
already in that camp, but I tend to be a perfectionist sometimes. I know it’s hard
to believe but sometimes I do.
If you’re steady
productive in four
hours of writing,
you’d get more done
than the average
marketer gets done
in a month.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 15 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 16
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
Dan used to preach “good enough is good enough.” That is really important.
Know when to stop and, in short, it’s not perfect. Perfect ads seldom work
anyway.
Often what you’re doing is you’re messing with
what’s going to be a 1% difference or less.
Dan: That’s right. It’s incremental.
John: So when it’s good enough, that’s when you pump it. That’s why Dan does
two edits and he’s done. I will do more but I’m working sometimes for clients
who demand that perfection. They don’t want the occasional typo in there.
We used to purposely put typos in our printed ads and our direct mail. So if it
was perfect it still didn’t look perfect but you can get that just by doing two less
edits usually.
Behind the One Legged Golfer
Dan: I know you’ve already talked about USP in the last session; however maybe
to revisit or extend or give you another bite at that apple, I think “One Legged
Golfer,” of course, is an all-time classic.
John: The process of getting there?
Dan: Yeah, I think you’re one of the best at getting to the big idea, the hidden
benefit, the hook, the thing that really makes a pitch stand apart from all other
pitches in its category.
John: The “One Legged Golfer” ad was a big breakthrough for me because of
two things.
One was I had a client who I wasn’t going to work for. I was actually having my
second or third midlife crisis and was playing biker bars in this band that I’d
formed. I didn’t want to work at all.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 16 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 17
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
So they lured me to come back to work by promising they would take the leash
off. What that meant was that they were going to run anything I wrote.
They would mail it and they would put it in the magazines and they would
swallow hard and they would not change a word.
To a writer who has worked with clients who are always changing stuff, that
was like catnip. It was just “okay” so we went and did it.
I just threw myself into this knowing that there was no limit to what I could do. I
could ruin these guys if I wanted to but my goal was to make them very
successful, which I was able to do.
And what I did is what Dan was talking about, the prep part.
I started interviewing clients and one of the first products they had was a golf
guy who had this strange swing that if you adopted the swing… and they were
selling videotapes, that’s going back, not even DVDs.
They just filmed this guy doing this swing and I
was supposed to sell this videotape, an
instructional golf video.
It was kind of new back then. This is probably
1991, something like that.
So I thought “That’s pretty boring. There’s not a
lot going on there. There’s nothing for me to get
my teeth around.”
So I interviewed what we call the talent, the guy behind the thing and I was
talking to him for about an hour on the phone. Wacky guy but no stories came
out.
Finally I said, “How did you come up with this triple coil swing?”
I could ruin these
guys if I wanted to
but my goal was to
make them very
successful.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 17 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 18
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
He says, “Oh, that old story. Nobody wants to hear that. I was golfing one day
behind a foursome. One of the guys only had one leg and, as he hopped to the
first tee to swing, I thought for sure he’s going to fall over but he didn’t. He had
this perfect swing and he knocked a 300 yard drive.”
I had this epiphany at the point and I just said, “Stop.” I said, “I’ve pretty much
got it. So this is what inspired you?” He says, “Yeah but nobody wants to hear
that stuff.”
Dan: Here’s what’s really important about this because we have this experience
with clients all the time.
They’ve got something spectacular and it’s old hat to them.
They don’t know it has any value, which means you have something like that, too.
I have yet to find anybody that doesn’t; with enough probing, they’re sitting on a
story they ought to be telling – that they either don’t like, they’re embarrassed by
yet they should be using it, they feel it’s trading on something – cancer recovery or
something like that. They don’t put any value on it.
Whatever. They’ve got it, which means you’ve got it.
John: This goes back to when I used to deal with clients. If I gave them an ad
and they said, “This is great. Can’t wait to run it,” I knew “bomb,” this thing was
not going to work.
I want a client so nervous that the only reason he runs
the ad is because he’s paying me so much money.
The sunk cost theory is eating away at him because they don’t want to do it.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 18 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 19
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
One of the ways you get to that is what Dan was just alluding to, I would
interview the secretaries, the salesmen – everybody else but the guy I was
dealing with – because I would get an edited dream-like story from the guy who
owned the company.
I want to hear the gossip, the rumors. I want to hear from the guys who have to
actually handle the refunds coming in.
I want to talk to the people who are doing customer support and often the best
stuff you’ll get is just right outside his office.
They’ll go, “Oh yeah he told you that? That’s bullshit.” And then the story will
bubble up.
Digging Deep
Dan: A few years ago I did a bunch of work for
Miracle Ear, the hearing aid company. They spent
$2 million with me, by the way, and used almost
nothing that I gave them.
John: A sure sign you did it right.
Dan: Oh yeah it was perfect. The franchisees that
did use it kicked butt but corporate set themselves on fire. But that’s neither here
or there.
Miracle Ear sells hearing aids; they’ve got about 1200 stores across the country.
The interesting thing about the company is the original founder, the guy that
started it, has sold it and bought it back for less than he sold it for; sold it, bought
it back… to big dumb companies, four times in his lifetime.
The only reason he’s not doing it a fifth time is he’s 92 years old.
I would get an edited
dream-like story
from the guy who
owned the company.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 19 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 20
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
He sells it to a big dumb company. They screw it all up. He buys it back. He fixes it.
He turns it back into a direct sales company and builds it back up, sells it to some
big dumb company.
When I was working for them they had just come out of being owned by Bausch &
Lomb who thought hearing aids were eyeglasses, which they aren’t. It’s more like
erectile dysfunction pills than it is eyeglasses for the male buyer. I mean it’s a big
emotional deal. It’s “we’re old.”
But here’s the deal… I’m interviewing franchise owners on the phone because I
don’t trust anything the corporate client is telling me. Just like I wouldn’t trust
anything you’re telling me – in part because you’ve got a belief system that builds
up about your business and your customers and so forth that you become
emotionally committed to, that often is not reality; in part because you’ve got
some asset like this that you don’t know you’ve
got, that you don’t value.
As John said, you’ve got to go poke around other
places and, if you’re doing it for yourself, you’ve
got to be willing to wipe the slate clean as best as
you can of all your biases.
Start doing the same – poking around as if you
have brought yourself in as an outsider… hard,
but not impossible.
I’m interviewing these franchise owners and I’m on the phone for an hour with
eight, 10 of them and the one I’m on the phone with used to be president of the
company at one time.
Now he’s retired and he’s got one little unit in Bum Lunk, Alabama and he plays a
lot of golf. So he starts telling me about the founder, which nobody has told me
about the founder. But he was president one of the times the founder was running
the company so he starts telling me about the founder.
You’ve got to be
willing to wipe the
slate clean as best as
you can of all your
biases.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 20 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 21
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
Now understand a hearing aid, especially to a male – you’re selling to a 60 to 80
year old guy – it’s a big emotional deal. These are people who don’t go to doctors
unless they’re drug there. They still are big self-reliance guys.
This is a big admission of weakness and frailty
and age that nobody wants to make.
So the founder of this company, his name is Ken Dahlberg, is a real life John Wayne
– he’s a World War II fighter pilot, shot down twice. Once escaped from France on
a bicycle under barbed wire, smuggled out by people who rescued him from the
field.
He’s got a Purple Heart. He’s got some award for shooting down more planes than
anybody else on the planet. He crawls out from under the barbed wire, gets home
and the first thing he does is sign up for the next mission. I mean this guy’s a real
life World War II hero. He’s still getting a royalty on the product because every
time he sells the company, he keeps a royalty. He’s still getting money so he’ll do
stuff.
First of all, they think he’s an idiot, which he isn’t. Second they think nobody wants
to hear that story. I’m hearing this I’m going, “That’s the voice, that’s the story.”
Now we can make this about doing something courageous – because he wears
them – that this is really a courageous thing to do,
not a weak thing to do.
We can put it in his voice.
They’re not using him. They don’t even have to go
rent a celebrity; they’ve got one and they’re not
even using the guy.
If I hadn’t made the phone calls and I hadn’t talked to the franchise owners, I
probably never would have found it because you had to go back in company
literature almost 50 years to find it being used.
Now we can make
this about doing
something
courageous.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 21 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 22
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
Gradually everybody around him convinced him to stop telling the story and he
didn’t want to tell the story anymore and so forth.
We find this again and again and again.
I promise you, you’ve got assets like this
that you aren’t leveraging.
It’s a good exercise amongst each other, by the way, in mastermind groups. It’s a
good place to do it – to probe each other.
John: One small caveat to that, the One Legged Golfer ad that I wrote has been
ripped off I think more than almost any other ad – and it’s still running by the
way. I still collect royalties on that every month.
That ad is now going on over 20 years old. It worked fine when the web came
along and it’s been in the mail and it still appears in tight markets.
But people rip it off and you can’t use that for an accountant. You can’t say, “The
Amazing Secrets of a One Legged Accountant That Will Help You Beat the IRS at
Their Own Game.” There has to be relevance.
So what Dan was talking about with this guy…, he found the hook, the hook
between this guy’s experience to the problem that people were having with
admitting that they needed to wear the hearing aids.
If you get that guy… and they never ran it? I can’t believe that they…
Dan: A lot of the work I did for them, some of the smaller franchise owners used it.
Corporate pretty much went in and backed out.
John: Go figure.
Dan: But anyway; clients are, you know, a “topic.”
John: All clients suck. I had that written above my desk for 20 years.
Dan: Some are worse than others but…
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 22 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 23
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
John: And when I started working for myself, I sucked. I was the worst client I
ever had in my life.
So, for entrepreneurs, just realize that. Suddenly all the stuff that I forced my
clients to do for 30 years ─ I wasn’t willing to do when I was doing my own stuff.
“I can’t use that headline. What about my reputation?”
Dan: I call it the “entrepreneurial transition.” That’s when you realize you have a
really bad manager and you have a really bad employee.
You are both of them and nobody can get fired. This is awful.
John: You’re related to both of them.
Dan: It’s worth always having at least one other employee so you have somebody
periodically you can fire or at least fantasize
about firing.
Biggest Breakthrough
So, in your entire career, biggest single discovery,
thing that surprised you, breakthrough…?
John: Well I’d have to say that was, for me, when
I started out and went to the Torrance Municipal
Library and read everything in the Dewey Decimal System, 600 to 820,
whatever it was.
The things that I pulled out of there that l liked were old books – and a lot of
them were out of print.
One of the books that really, really hit me hard was Claude Hopkins’ “Scientific
Advertising” and “My Life in Advertising,” It’s almost always published as a
single book but it’s two different books he wrote in 1921.
Even then, when I found it, to me this was an insight into the kind of
salesmanship that I wasn’t finding. Because the other books I was pulling off of
When I started
working for myself, I
sucked. I was the
worst client I ever
had in my life.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 23 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 24
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
the shelf were much more recent, more like textbooks and they talked about
stuff that sounded good but it was theory. It wasn’t how to do it.
And here’s this guy from half a century ago that was telling me all this.
What happened was that me liking that became my entry into meeting Jay
Abraham and Gary Halbert and Joe Sugarman; those were their favorite books,
too.
We had this little cabal of people who the things that we were using were books
that were out of print.
When I was working with Gary, we paid $1,000 for a third generation
mimeograph of “The Robert Collier Letter Book.”
I don’t know if that’s one of the ones you
recommend.
Dan: Yeah, and now you can get it at Amazon by
the way.
John: Well what happened was, when we
started doing the seminars, guys like Galletti
came out and they found they got these for
nothing and are now publishing them. So they’re all back in print.
But this stuff was out of print. Howard made that trek to the National Library of
Congress and spent weeks in there reading out of print books that you couldn’t
otherwise find. He just found these things by John E. Kennedy… the original
guys.
Just the discovery, that was my connection to these guys and of course the next
intellectual thing is that really means there’s a connection between us and the
cavemen who were selling stuff to each other.
I forget who it was that found in an obscure text that there were used chariot
salesmen in Rome.
When I found it, this
was an insight into
the kind of
salesmanship that I
wasn’t finding in
other books.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 24 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 25
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
Dan: People think USA Today was the first national newspaper. There was a
national newspaper during the Civil War called Harper’s Weekly – not to be
confused with Harper’s Magazine, which came later.
It was a national newspaper like USA Today is today. I have replica issues; you can
buy replica issues now of this newspaper.
The classified section is full of lead generation ads for “Get Rich in Real Estate,”
“How to Get a Job in Sales” and stuff that you could buy and resell and be a direct
sales person and make money; then direct response ads for baldness cures.
There’s an ad in a Civil War era Harper’s Newspaper for the electric vibrating hair
brush, which stimulates your follicles and somehow regrows hair.
There is a current ad in the Sharper Image catalog for the same stupid product
that doesn’t do anything. And, by the way, if you lay it side-by-side with the
Harper’s ad from the Civil War, there’s enormous similarity; however, the Civil
War ad is better.
So these fundamentals… and that’s the thing that should be really encouraging to
everybody, especially anybody that’s new to info-marketing or to direct marketing,
is there are “locked in place fundamentals” that you can absolutely rely on, that
really nothing has changed.
People always think the next new media changes the rules. We’ve lived long
enough so that we’ve been through this with media that has come and gone.
John: Remember 900 numbers?
Dan: 900 numbers. That was like everybody talking about Facebook now.
Everybody was talking about 900 numbers – there were seminars, there were
training programs, there were speakers running around teaching.
John: We told them over and over again, “It’s going to be sex lines, guys.” But
they all wanted to have latest sports scores, weather.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 25 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 26
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
Dan: Which pretty much, by the way still is what e-commerce is, too. It’s 80% sex
and 20% the rest of us.
The Online Impact
So here’s my question – specific to all the online media – how do you feel your
skills, your methodology, your approach is antiquated by any of that or has to be
altered by any of that? Has it changed any of the rules you live by?
John: Yes, the physical rules. I cut my teeth as an “A list” copywriter by writing
for the largest mailers in the United States like Rodale and things like that.
I got to the point where I would start writing and, when I was done, I would
either have an 8-page or a 12-page or a 16-page direct mail letter and it was
because I just trained myself so long.
The reason you go eight or 12 or 16 is because that’s how they were printed. It’s
called the four page signature – it would actually
go through the physical print. It couldn’t be a 15-
pager because that would be a blank page on the
back.
If I went one page over, I think it was four pages
plus a brochure and some other things, it would pretty much be an ounce and
then you’d pay for each little thing that would go above that. So the physical
nature of that was very real.
It was the same with newspaper ads, of course. You had a certain amount of
space. You could only drop the point size so far before it became unreadable and
you kept packing stuff in there.
The web removed that to a large degree, much to the chagrin of the futures for a
lot of marketers around 2004 that were actually selling a lot online.
People always think
the next new media
changes the rules.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 26 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 27
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
They were newbies in markets. There was low hanging fruit. They were writing
hundred page sales letters because they just didn’t know when to stop. There
was no deadline. There was no reason to stop.
They could write whatever they want. One URL could be as many pages as you
could fit. So it erased that.
I find that, when I write for online things in a print ad, it still comes down to
what is essentially an eight or 12 page letter because I can cover everything I
need to know. So the discipline is gone. That would be the main thing.
Video sales letters actually have reintroduced discipline because you can’t go on
forever in a video, although…
Dan: “End of America,” 92 minutes, you can go a long way, but…
John: A lot of the ones that are working now, the last I heard was 29 minutes on
average for direct response sales videos – not lead gen but sales videos. I think it
was Jon Benson was talking about the 29 minutes. I may be wrong by a few
minutes. That’s a long time but it is still a concise
time and they got there from testing. They tested
it out.
So there are differences, kind of like one thing I
learned from Dan was when infomercials came
around. That was a new medium that came. It
was on TV.
It wasn’t new to advertise on TV and
infomercials actually went back to the birth of the medium.
The early shows of the ‘50s were all infomercials. The Johnny Carson was an
infomercial, the dancing cigarette boxes.
I better not talk about this stuff. You guys have no idea what I’m talking about.
But infomercials weren’t new. But the form that came about because of cable TV
Video sales letters
actually have
reintroduced
discipline because
you can’t go on
forever in a video.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 27 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 28
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
did change things because there were concise periods of time to get in. And you
were like one of the inventors of the thing.
Dan: Close.
John: But you were there with the guys who invented the blue screen. And then
they decided a blue screen has to appear every 15 minutes and then it was every
10 minutes. They started testing and pretty soon it became very concise.
The first infomercials I wrote… we had a client, Gary and I, they bought scrap TV
time at a channel in Los Angeles. We went in and wrote the thing as we went,
had a set there, the guy would bring on, you know who this guy is.
Dan: Hal Morris.
John: Yeah. This guy would bring on a guy to sell something and he’d just talk to
him and when they were done… because they knew they had a 45 minute or an
hour or a 29 minute slot to fill for free that night on BET so they would stop.
The first one I saw, Hal started talking to the guy and he goes, “Wait we’re
recording?” And he goes, “Yeah, yeah come on. Go.” They left that in there. No
editing. Went straight from there, and when it was over he said, “Okay that’s
done.”
They slammed it up on BET that night for free because BET didn’t realize that
time was so valuable. It was 2:00am. And they thought, “Nobody’s going to pay
or watch at 2:00am. You can have it.”
So that was a wild period of time. But then, from that “no rules,” it started.
People tested and the cable networks realized that that time was very valuable.
So you see these things go through this.
The web has gone through that same process – now with it being much more
circumscribed from testing by the way, which is the essence of direct response.
Dan: Well that’s the best thing about it, is it’s a great place to test.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 28 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 29
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
John: Or cheaper. But I still run it.
Dan: It’s only indicative, it’s not predictive is what I always say; because, if you
move something from one media to the next, it may have worked or not worked in
one but it could have life in another.
John: I get so many clients though that are online; that come to me and they
brag about how big their list is. It’s like, “I’ve got 70,000 people on my list.”
“How many of those are buyers?” “I don’t know.”
I became a cash millionaire with a list of 300
people at first.
Get the numbers right, get the math right, that’s
what’s going on. What did you say it was?
Dan: Half behavioral psych, half math. Most
people don’t like the math by the way. They don’t
like that part of the process but the math is
everything.
John: You don't have to be a math whiz but you need to know what numbers
count and what don't.
I still get questions like, “What percentage response should I expect to get from
my whatever?”
I don't know. If you sell something for a million dollars, you only need one
response out of whatever size your list is.
Dan: It's one of the all-time dumbest questions. It's the most frequently asked
question. It doesn't matter. Only ROI matters.
But most clients – including good, smart clients – I can usually stump them with
five questions.
You don't have to be
a math whiz but you
need to know what
numbers count and
what don't.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 29 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 30
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
It's just amazing how few people know
the numbers of their business.
What does it cost you to get a customer from source A versus source B?
What was the customer worth over 12 months from source A versus source B?
People don't know this stuff. And they're making decisions in the dark.
John: When you had that slide earlier of the test, headline A with offer B, when
you do that how many different permutations did that come out to? It was
massive.
Dan: Well I just stopped but you can very easily have a matrix with 200, 250
different…
John: But what's interesting is that you get somebody who's all math and they
will take a bad headline with a bad offer and do all this testing and keep coming
up with zero.
So sell one thing first, move through and that's where the guys that know the
behavioral psych understand they can have a really good offer and a really good
headline.
But what happens with testing is that you get an old school guy who doesn't
want to do the numbers and somebody else comes in and actually says, "Did you
try testing against that headline or that offer?" And he says, "No, I go by my gut."
Those tests usually prove that guy wrong at some point.
All ads tire out. So you can never rely on a single ad.
You've got to get hip to this stuff. Behavioral psychology and math.
Dan: Stuff wears out faster because of online and in online. Not always but a lot of
times.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 30 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 31
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
John: That's a major problem I ran into as a freelancer, too. When you're
running an ad, you will get tired of it before the masses tire out.
Let the math tell you when it's time to change the ad or do something. Don't try
to circumvent the natural life cycle.
Dan: We had my “Gold by the Inch” show on for eight and half years and after six
months almost every week I had a conversation with Len where he's calling up,
"We got to do a new show." I said, "Why do we got to do a new show?"
"Well the numbers are same." "Then why do we have to have a new show?”
“Everybody's seen it."
I said, "No. If you would quit getting up at 3:00 in the morning to watch your own
damn show, you would not think everybody's seen it."
We ran that show for eight and a half years
without a single change in it and still making
money to the bitter end.
Major Influences
Last question, you kind of went there with some
authors but people often ask and it's a good
question… Two, three, four major influencers of
how you approach selling and copy that people might want to Amazon, Google, go
hunt for in a used bookstore, that sort of thing?
John: I was really lucky and I happened to live in Los Angeles at a time when
the New York agencies opened up direct response branches in Los Angeles.
It was a golden age back in the early ‘80s. They're all out of business now. But I
was the guy that would sneak in the back door because their staff didn't
understand direct response and they didn't want the clients to see me because I
am who I am. So they’d sneak me in and I’d do that writing.
Let the math tell you
when it's time to
change the ad or do
something.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 31 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 32
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
But living down there at that time was Jay Abraham and I met him because he
pissed me off and I went to his office to hit him.
He had run an ad in the Los Angeles Times looking for direct response
copywriters and I sent one.
He sent me back a form letter saying, "You really need to read Claude Hopkins if
you're going to get into this biz."
I had already devoured everything of that. So I was really ticked off that he
hadn't even looked at my letter or my stuff.
We wound up being great friends and I traded hanging around his office for
doing work for him. I worked for free but my payment was being able to hang
out there. So I got to watch him do consultations.
That was the first time I saw… this was a long time ago when $2,000 meant
something. People paid $2,000 an hour to fly into Los Angeles and go and sit in
his office while he would sometimes exercise and just pontificate.
It was worth it at the time because there was no
other source of that stuff.
At Jay's divorce party I met Gary Halbert. Gary
Halbert, when I met him, was the most arrogant,
dismissive bastard I had ever met in my life and I
liked him immediately.
We became fast friends. He was a mentor and then we worked together and we
were lifelong friends until he died a couple of years ago.
And through Jay I met John Finn. He was the only agent for direct response
freelance copywriters in the world. He just invented it. He said, "Somebody
needs to be an agent."
I worked for free but
my payment was
being able to hang
out there.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 32 of 33 --

HOW TO CREATE IRRESISTIBLE SALES MESSAGES FOR ANY BUSINESS PAGE 33
© 2014 GKIC Module 3
So I met him and he started hooking me up with people and I started
ghostwriting for a couple of writers who became very, very famous.
One is Jim Rutz, who is a very interesting guy, but the thing about Jim is that he
taught me discipline. I ghost wrote for him essentially. He got the deal and we
brainstormed but I did all the grunt work of writing the thing.
He made me do 16 versions of an ad that was almost there. It was good enough
but it wasn't perfect and he needed it to be perfect because he was going up
against other A-list writers.
In that game with A-list writers at places like
Boardroom and Rodale and Agora – because they
have such good writers – you need to be a little
more perfect than the average entrepreneur
because you're going to win by a .01% and that will become the control and you
can lose by .01% and a near perfect letter can become trash meat just because it
got beat by a…
Dan: That's why those are the guys you really want to pay attention to. You want
to be buying from Rodale. You want to be buying from Boardroom. You want to be
buying from Agora because they're getting the top talent.
John: I'd say at that time there were 15 direct response writers worthy of
writing for those guys in the world.
There's probably a few more now but it always seems to settle down to just a
handful that figure it out and are willing to do that.
But you don't need to be A-list. You need to understand what they're doing. You
don't need to approach that but it's always good to know what the best in the
biz are doing.
It's always good to
know what the best
in the biz are doing.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 33 of 33 --
